expectations.text: Uses useWriteContracts hook (not useSendCalls or custom encoding)
expectations.evidence: In packages/nextjs/app/batch-transfer/page.tsx line 8: `import { useCapabilities, useWriteContracts } from "wagmi/experimental"` and line 62: `const { writeContractsAsync } = useWriteContracts()`. No useSendCalls or custom encoding found anywhere in the codebase.

expectations.text: Uses useCapabilities for wallet EIP-5792 support detection
expectations.evidence: In page.tsx line 8: imported from `wagmi/experimental`, and lines 21-23: `const { isSuccess: isEIP5792Wallet, data: walletCapabilities } = useCapabilities({ account: connectedAddress })`. The result is used to derive `isBatchingSupported` on line 161.

expectations.text: Uses useShowCallsStatus for batch transaction status display
expectations.evidence: useShowCallsStatus is not imported or used anywhere in the implementation files. Grep of the entire packages/ directory returns no matches. The implementation uses a manual `isBatchPending` state variable with loading spinner instead.

expectations.text: Provides graceful fallback for wallets without EIP-5792 support
expectations.evidence: Lines 87-117 implement individual approve and transfer handlers using `useScaffoldWriteContract`. Lines 311-333 render a dedicated 'Individual Transactions (Fallback)' card with separate Approve and Transfer buttons. Lines 302-306 show a note when batching is not supported. The UI explicitly labels this as fallback for non-EIP-5792 wallets.

expectations.text: Batch button conditionally disabled when wallet doesn't support EIP-5792
expectations.evidence: Line 291: `disabled={isBatchPending || !recipientAddress || !transferAmount}` — the batch button is NOT disabled based on `isBatchingSupported`. The button remains clickable even when EIP-5792 is not detected. A warning note appears below (lines 302-306), but the button itself is not disabled.

expectations.text: ERC20 smart contract with approve+transfer pattern created
expectations.evidence: packages/hardhat/contracts/BatchToken.sol extends OpenZeppelin's ERC20 (`import "@openzeppelin/contracts/token/ERC20/ERC20.sol"`), which provides approve(), transfer(), transferFrom(), allowance() and balanceOf(). The frontend uses approve and transfer functions in both batch and individual modes (lines 93-96, 110-112, 134-147).

packages/hardhat/deploy/01_deploy_batch_token.ts exists with proper hardhat-deploy pattern: imports HardhatRuntimeEnvironment and DeployFunction types, uses `hre.deployments.deploy('BatchToken', ...)`, and sets `deployBatchToken.tags = ['BatchToken']`.

expectations.text: Frontend page with batch UI created
expectations.evidence: packages/nextjs/app/batch-transfer/page.tsx is a 339-line Next.js page with: token info card, wallet capability detection card, mint section, transfer setup with AddressInput, batch approve+transfer button (EIP-5792), and individual fallback buttons. Navigation link added in Header.tsx.

expectations.text: Uses SE-2 scaffold hooks for contract interaction
expectations.evidence: Line 9 imports `useDeployedContractInfo, useScaffoldReadContract, useScaffoldWriteContract` from `~~/hooks/scaffold-eth`. useScaffoldReadContract is used 5 times (name, symbol, decimals, balanceOf, allowance). useScaffoldWriteContract is used for mint, approve, and transfer fallback. useDeployedContractInfo fetches contract ABI/address for the batch call.

expectations.text: No new npm dependencies needed (wagmi already has EIP-5792 hooks)
expectations.evidence: No changes to any package.json file (git diff shows no modifications). The implementation imports from `wagmi/experimental` which is part of the existing wagmi dependency. No `yarn add` commands were run.

claims.claim: Solidity compilation was successful (8 files compiled)
claims.evidence: Summary states compilation was successful. The contract is valid Solidity extending OpenZeppelin ERC20.

claims.claim: Next.js production build was successful with /batch-transfer route at 3.14 kB
claims.evidence: Summary states build was successful. The page.tsx file exists and uses valid React/Next.js patterns.
Summary states no TypeScript errors, consistent with the build succeeding.

claims.claim: Uses useShowCallsStatus for batch transaction status
claims.evidence: The summary does NOT claim to use useShowCallsStatus, but the skill documentation mentions it as an expected pattern. The implementation uses manual isBatchPending state instead.

suggestions.assertion: Batch button conditionally disabled when wallet doesn't support EIP-5792
suggestions.reason: This is a debatable design choice. Some implementations intentionally keep the batch button enabled for all wallets because wagmi's useWriteContracts can fall back to sequential transactions internally. The expectation could be reworded to 'UI clearly communicates EIP-5792 support status' which the implementation does achieve via the badge and note.

suggestions.assertion: Uses useShowCallsStatus for batch transaction status display
suggestions.reason: This hook may not be strictly necessary if the implementation provides its own status management. Consider whether the eval should accept alternative status display approaches (like the loading spinner used here) as valid, or if the specific hook is required for a particular UX reason.
No assertion checks whether the implementation compiles and builds successfully, which is a fundamental quality gate. The summary claims it does, but the eval doesn't verify it.

eval_feedback.overall: Assertions are mostly well-targeted. The two failures reflect genuine gaps — useShowCallsStatus is entirely absent, and the batch button lacks EIP-5792-conditional disabling. Consider adding a build-verification assertion since that catches many real-world failures.
